mission.title: Install and Configure Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK)
mission.description: Elastic Cloud on Kubernetes (ECK) automates the deployment and management of Elastic Stack components on Kubernetes. It is ideal for users looking to run Elasticsearch, Kibana, and other Elastic products in a Kubernetes environment. Key trade-offs include the need for Kubernetes expertise and potential complexity in configuration.

steps.title: Add the ECK Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the Elastic Helm repository to your Helm client, allowing you to install ECK from the official source.

```bash

helm repo add elastic https://helm.elastic.co

helm repo update

```

> This adds the Elastic Helm repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
CRDs are necessary for ECK to manage Elastic Stack resources. This step installs them into your Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml

```

> This installs the required CRDs for ECK.

steps.title: Install ECK Operator
steps.description:
This step installs the ECK operator in the 'elastic-system' namespace, which will manage your Elastic Stack resources.

```bash

helm install eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --create-namespace --version 2.14.0

```

> Installs the ECK operator with the specified version.

steps.title: Create an Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step creates an Elasticsearch cluster using a Custom Resource (CR) definition, which ECK will manage.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: data
EOF

```

> Creates a 3-node Elasticsearch cluster.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: data
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Elasticsearch cluster
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the Elasticsearch cluster to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get elasticsearch -n elastic-system

```

> Verify that the Elasticsearch cluster is in a 'Green' state.

uninstall.title: Uninstall ECK Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the ECK operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if the Elasticsearch cluster is not backed up.

```bash

helm uninstall eck-operator --namespace elastic-system

kubectl delete namespace elastic-system

```

> Uninstalls the ECK operator.
> Deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade ECK Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your Elasticsearch data. This step upgrades the ECK operator to a newer version.

```bash

kubectl drain <node-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade eck-operator elastic/eck-operator --namespace elastic-system --version 2.14.0

kubectl get elasticsearch -n elastic-system

```

> Cordon and drain the node before upgrading.
> Upgrades the ECK operator to the specified version.
> Verify that the Elasticsearch cluster is still running correctly after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Elasticsearch cluster is not starting
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Insufficient resources allocated to the nodes.

**Fix:**
Increase the resource limits in the Elasticsearch CR definition.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to connect to Elasticsearch'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Elasticsearch service is not reachable due to network policies.

**Fix:**
Check and update network policies to allow traffic to the Elasticsearch service.

troubleshooting.title: Kibana is not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** Kibana pod is not running or has crashed.

**Fix:**
Check the Kibana pod logs for errors and restart the pod if necessary.

troubleshooting.title: CRD not found error
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** CRDs were not installed correctly.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://download.elastic.co/downloads/eck/2.14.0/crds.yaml.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Elasticsearch 8.6.0, improved TLS certificate management.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Elasticsearch versions below 7.10.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from 2.12 or 2.13, ensure to update your Elasticsearch CR definitions to match the new version requirements.

resolution.summary: You should see the Elasticsearch cluster in a 'Green' state when running: kubectl get elasticsearch -n elastic-system.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: elasticsearch.k8s.elastic.co/v1
kind: Elasticsearch
metadata:
  name: my-elasticsearch
  namespace: elastic-system
spec:
  version: 8.6.0
  nodeSets:
  - name: default
    count: 3
    config:
      node.store.type: data
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure your Kubernetes cluster meets the resource requirements and that you have the necessary tools installed.
